title: Cloudflare DDNS Client
subtitle: Self-hosted dynamic DNS updater with live web dashboard
description: A containerised Dynamic DNS client that keeps Cloudflare DNS records in sync with your real public IP — with smart polling, rate-limit awareness, and a live web dashboard to manage everything without touching config files.
overview: Cloudflare DDNS Client is a self-hosted solution for anyone running services on a dynamic IP. It extends the popular timothyjmiller/cloudflare-ddns project with a full web dashboard, a REST API, smart IP polling, and Docker-first packaging. Two processes run side-by-side in one container: the DDNS daemon and a Flask/Gunicorn dashboard server — independently updating DNS and serving live status with zero extra Cloudflare API calls.
challenge: Home servers and self-hosted stacks live on dynamic IPs — the moment your ISP rotates the address, your DNS records go stale and services go dark. Existing clients were either too primitive (no visibility into what was happening) or hammered the Cloudflare API so aggressively they risked being rate-limited. The goal was near-real-time IP sync with full observability, all inside a single, easy-to-deploy container.
solution: The daemon polls your public IP every 2–5 seconds using plain HTTP (multiple independent providers, never the Cloudflare API), and only calls Cloudflare when the IP actually changes. A built-in sliding-window rate limiter ensures the 1,200 calls/5 min ceiling is never approached. The Flask dashboard reads a status.json file written after every poll — live data at zero API cost. The UI lets you add/delete subdomains, import existing Cloudflare records, and trigger manual updates, all without editing config files.

title: Agendash 3
subtitle: Enhanced job-scheduling dashboard for Agenda.js — published to npm
description: A significantly enhanced fork of Agendash — the dashboard for Agenda.js — with a full UI overhaul, per-job execution logging stored in MongoDB, and support for Express, Hapi, Koa and Fastify. Published to npm as agendash3-rework.
overview: Agendash 3 is an open-source dashboard for Agenda.js, the Node.js job-scheduling library. The original Agendash was functional but visually dated and had no execution history — when jobs failed silently, there was nothing to look at. This fork modernises the entire UI and adds structured logging for every job lifecycle event, packaged as a drop-in npm middleware compatible with the four major Node.js HTTP frameworks.
challenge: The original Agendash had no observability beyond job status. Silent failures left developers querying MongoDB directly to understand what went wrong. The frontend was also tightly coupled, unresponsive on mobile, and difficult to extend. On top of that, any logging solution needed its own isolated Mongoose connection to avoid polluting the host application's database layer.
solution: Rebuilt the entire frontend with Bootstrap 4/5 — responsive layouts, cleaner job lists, improved modals with loading states, and a dedicated execution-logs panel per job. On the backend, Agenda's start, complete, and fail events are intercepted and written to a tasklogs MongoDB collection through a dedicated Mongoose connection passed at init time, keeping it completely independent from the host app. Automatic index creation on the ready event removes manual DB setup. Published as agendash3-rework on npm for easy drop-in use.

title: OpenAI Cost Calculator
subtitle: Maintained npm package for accurate OpenAI API cost estimation
description: A maintained fork of openai-cost-calculator — an npm module that calculates costs across all OpenAI model categories (language, fine-tuning, embedding, image, audio) with pricing kept current as OpenAI's catalogue evolves.
overview: openai-token-cost-calculator-updated is a lightweight npm utility for developers building on top of the OpenAI API who need to estimate or track spend. It covers the full model surface — GPT-4o, GPT-4 Turbo, fine-tuning runs, embeddings, image generation, and Whisper audio — returning structured cost breakdowns per call. The package is a maintained fork of the original codergautam/openai-cost-calculator, kept up to date as OpenAI releases new models and revises pricing.
challenge: OpenAI updates its model lineup and pricing regularly. The original package had fallen behind — missing GPT-4o, newer embedding models, and updated token prices — which meant developers relying on it were silently getting wrong cost estimates. There was no reliable, actively-maintained alternative on npm.
solution: Forked the original, audited and corrected all existing pricing, and added support for every current model category including GPT-4o-mini, gpt-4-turbo, text-embedding-3 variants, TTS and TTS HD. Exposed five clean functions — one per model type — each returning a structured object with prompt cost, completion cost, total, and a formatted string. Published as openai-token-cost-calculator-updated and kept in sync with OpenAI announcements.

title: Original Portfolio
subtitle: My first portfolio — vanilla HTML, CSS & JavaScript SPA
description: The portfolio that started it all. A single-page application built with zero frameworks — pure HTML, CSS, and JavaScript — with a particle background, Swiper.js slider, and a working contact form.
overview: This is where the journey began. Built in 2023 during my studies, this was my first attempt at a real web presence — a three-section SPA (About, Work, Contacts) written entirely from scratch with no frameworks, no build tools, just raw HTML, CSS, and vanilla JS. Looking back at it now against this portfolio is a clear measure of how far things have come.
challenge: Starting from scratch with no framework, no component model, and no real-world project experience. Every layout decision, every animation, every form interaction had to be figured out manually. The goal was simply to ship something live that represented me as a developer.
solution: Kept the scope tight and deliberate — three sections, clean layout, one visual flourish (Particles.js background animation), and a Swiper.js slider for the work section. Wired up a real contact form via un-static.com. Deployed via GitHub Pages. No dependencies beyond two focused libraries. The constraint of no frameworks forced a solid understanding of how the DOM, events, and CSS actually work.

title: Tic-Tac-Toe vs AI
subtitle: Desktop game with minimax + alpha-beta pruning AI
description: A C# WinForms Tic-Tac-Toe game where the AI opponent is powered by minimax with alpha-beta pruning — unbeatable by design, with move-time analytics and score tracking across sessions.
overview: One of the first real projects — a Human vs AI Tic-Tac-Toe desktop application built in C# with Windows Forms. The focus wasn't the game itself but the AI behind it: a full minimax implementation with alpha-beta pruning that searches the entire game tree and plays a theoretically perfect game every time. Built as a way to deeply understand recursive algorithms and game theory from first principles.
challenge: Implementing an AI that genuinely cannot be beaten, not just one that plays randomly or checks for obvious wins. Understanding and coding minimax from scratch — the recursive evaluation of every possible game state, assigning scores, backtracking — was the core difficulty. Alpha-beta pruning was then layered on to cut down the unnecessary branches the algorithm explored.
solution: The board is modelled as a Player[,] array with an enum for X (human), O (AI), and None. The minimax function recursively evaluates all possible moves, maximising the AI score and minimising the player's, with alpha-beta pruning discarding branches that can't affect the final decision. A special first-move heuristic makes the AI prioritise adjacent positions before invoking full minimax. Every AI move is timed with a Stopwatch and displayed in a DataGridView for performance analytics.

title: Battleship vs AI
subtitle: Desktop naval strategy game with hunt-and-target AI
description: A C# WinForms Battleship game with a two-phase hunt-and-target AI. Two 10×10 grids, full ship placement with boundary and overlap validation, visual hover preview, and a smart AI that switches from random hunting to targeted follow-up the moment it scores a hit.
overview: Built alongside the Tic-Tac-Toe project in 2023, this was a step up in complexity — a full Battleship game with two interactive 10×10 grids, ship placement logic, and an AI opponent that actually plays intelligently. Minimax is impractical for Battleship (the state space is enormous), so the AI uses the classic Hunt/Target strategy used by strong human players — the same approach that forms the basis of competitive Battleship AI.
challenge: Battleship's hidden-information nature makes minimax impossible to apply directly — the AI cannot see the player's board. The challenge was designing an AI that feels genuinely threatening without full information: it needed to search efficiently during the hunting phase and close out ships quickly once a hit was scored.
solution: The AI runs two phases. In the hunting phase it picks random untried cells, statistically favouring patterns that maximise coverage. Once a hit is registered, it switches to the targeting phase — using GetConsecutiveButtons() and GetAdjacentButtons() to detect row or column alignment from consecutive hits, then systematically extending along the axis until the ship sinks. Ship state is tracked in dictionaries keyed by size, and the player side includes a full hover-preview placement system with boundary and overlap validation.
